title: Last Shift
short: Two worker subjects on an industrial deck. One sits against a pipe. The other kneels making hand contact.
unlock: The healthy worker stayed at the side of the fallen one. That's the part that matters. No regulations required it, and it certainly didn't fit into their work schedule. One person couldn't continue, so the other remained to comfort. And all around them, the station's operations were neglected and fell apart.

title: Still Pumping
short: An adult subject kneels beside a child subject. The adult subject gestures toward machinery. The child subject wears an oversized protective helmet.
unlock: The helmet is too large. That means the lesson is too heavy. The adult is showing the child the machine because the machine has become part of their family's survival. It's something to understand, fear, and keep in operation at all costs.

title: Burner Light
short: Three adult subjects around a small flame source. One seated. One stretching hands over the fire. One standing in a posture of alertness.
unlock: They gathered around the burner because it still gave off heat. It didn't provide comfort exactly. Just a little heat. Enough to make a circle. Enough for one person to rest, one to warm their hands, and one to keep watch in the darkness.

title: Open Container
short: One adult subject and two child subjects positioned around an open cargo container. Container interior used as a shelter space.
unlock: They left the door open so the container would feel less like storage. Less like they had been packed away. One child has already crawled inside. One still stands at the threshold. The parent offers rations to them, as if trying to demonstrate that this arrangement can function, that a steel box can become a living space if people tried hard enough.

title: Ocean Funeral
short: Two adult subjects at a platform rail dropping a wrapped bundle. The bundle falls beyond the platform boundary into open water.
unlock: On the drowned Earth, there are no solemn funeral processions. No decorated graves. No hallowed ground. Just two people bidding farewell. They're giving their dead to the very same water that took everything else.

title: Numbered Door
short: A worker subject points at the access point to a storage container while three subjects of varying size stand with carrying bags.
unlock: This is housing reduced to allocation. A number on a steel door becomes kitchen, bedroom, shelter, and future. The MOSES official only needs to point. The family has no choice but accept the decision.

title: Water Queue
short: Three adult subjects stand in stationary queue formation. Each carries a water container. Collection point appears damaged or improvised.
unlock: Nothing dramatic is happening here. There's no running or shouting. Just three people giving part of their lives to a pipe that may or may not answer their plea for water. When a whole day can be spent waiting for one bucket of water, that's when you know survival defines human existence.

title: Bed on Wheels
short: Two adult subjects transport a third prone subject on a stretcher through a corridor. The stretcher appears improvised. Medical destination unknown.
unlock: They're moving a hospital stretcher through a hallway because rooms no longer mean what they're supposed to mean... a secure place. The sick person is still being moved with care, and that matters. Even when systems and civilizations fail, people still keep trying to deliver each other to someplace safer.

title: Shared Blanket
short: Two adult subjects in close contact with each other and seated against a wall. A single covering inadequately covers both bodies.
unlock: A single blanket can't fix anything. It can't make food appear, restart power, or bring rescue closer. What it can do is hold the heat generated by two human bodies. When people have almost nothing left to give, they'll still give warmth.

title: Carried Body
short: Two adult subjects carry an inactive third subject through a corridor. The carried subject shows no independent movement.
unlock: This isn't ceremony. It's work that happens when ceremony isn't possible any longer. That corridor must be kept open. People still need to pass. So, two survivors carry the dead with whatever care they can still manage. All the while, life continues around a space that should've been allowed to stop and reflect.

title: Thirsty Wait
short: Multiple subjects of varying size gathered around an inactive water-processing unit. They hold water containers in a posture of waiting. One subject appears engaged in repair efforts.
unlock: Nobody is doing anything here. They're all waiting, and that's the point. The whole day has been reduced to the narrow hope that one machine will start working again. Every bucket in that line is a reminder that thirst never feels dramatic in the beginning. It's a growing presence that waits alongside you and never leaves.

title: Ration Split
short: Single adult subject dividing a food portion between two seated child subjects. Distribution action careful. Quantity insufficient for long-term sustenance.
unlock: There's no correct way to divide an insufficient amount of food. The adult isn't solving the hunger problem, only choosing how best to avoid disappointing either child. She anguishes over the choice, but she still makes it with great care. Even love is forced to become arithmetic when survival is at stake because numbers never forgive.

title: Hydroponic Check
short: Two adult subjects tend a small cultivation bed. One subject kneels in a posture of inspection. The other directs a light source.
unlock: Their hydroponics tray is quite small, but both people act like it contains their entire future. The person holding the light isn't trying make the plants more visible. They're using it as a makeshift grow light. They're desperately trying to ensure that food can still grow under human care. A tiny garden becomes enormous when all other gardens have failed.

title: Haircut Corner
short: One seated child subject. One standing adult subject performing fine hand motions near the child subject's head region. Grooming activity a strong possibility.
unlock: Cutting a child's hair does nothing to improve their chances of survival, but that's what makes the act meaningful. The parent still wanted the child to feel seen, clean, and still human. Even as the world collapses, this family made time to groom themselves. They didn't do it for their bodies, but for their souls, so that they wouldn't go feral.

title: Measuring Wall
short: Adult subject positions a child subject upright against a vertical surface. Mark applied to the wall at the child subject's head.
unlock: This family lived trapped in a place meant to be temporary. Yet, their child kept growing anyway. So, they marked the growth milestones on the wall, because growth is a kind of defiance against despair. The world continued to narrow into a terrible place of waiting, shortages, and fear. Yet, the child still grew taller. Someone needed to notice that.

title: Radio Call
short: An adult subject positioned in front of a communication device. Head and hand orientation indicate an active transmission or listening state. No response detected.
unlock: The person speaks into the radio because hearing silence is worse than continuing to try. A radio gives hope a process: press, call, wait, and listen. The cruel part is that waiting feels like an action, too. For a little while, people can make themselves believe that an answer is only delayed for some reason, instead of gone forever.

title: Signal Flag
short: An adult subject stands on the top of a container structure, moving a cloth attached to a rod in a repeated signaling pattern. A second adult subject watches from below.
unlock: Against the vastness of the ocean, a flag only appears as a tiny speck. However, the survivor waves it anyway because giving up is another kind of death. A companion watches from below, not because they know help is coming, but because hope sometimes needs a witness in order to keep trying.

title: Passing Boat
short: Three adult subjects positioned at the edge of a platform. All subjects direct arm signals toward something unseen by the scan.
unlock: These people are waving to a passing boat. This is the moment rescue becomes visible, but still doesn't arrive. That may be worse than never seeing it at all. The people wave because they believe the boat hasn't noticed them. It has. But, they keep waving because accepting that truth would mean the end of hope.

title: Final Cast
short: An child subject seated at the edge of a platform. A line object extends from the subject's hands into the water beyond the boundary of the scan.
unlock: The boy isn't fishing because the fishing is good here. The world's biosphere is dying. He's fishing to give his waiting a purpose. As long as the line remains in the water, there's still a chance to catch some food, small as it may be. Pulling the line in empty means facing the reality of starvation.

title: Dead Signal
short: An adult subject positioned at an inactive communication terminal. One hand maintains physical contact with the screen surface. No active signals detected.
unlock: The screen is dead, but the person keeps a hand there anyway. Humans do this when a connection is gone but their muscle memory still remembers where it used to be. Touch becomes the last message. Not sent, not received, but held in place because letting go makes the silence total and complete.

title: Retrieval
short: Three worker subjects exit a storage chamber with a sealed seed case. Handling posture indicates high caution, high assigned value, and possible ritual significance.
unlock: They're not carrying ordinary cargo. They're carrying unborn lives with a care usually reserved for the dead or the divine. One advances carefully, while the others look back.  They know they're leaving behind far more than they can save.

title: Closed Door
short: One worker subject stands at a sealed chamber door with head and hand against the barrier. No active override attempt detected. Posture suggests hesitation, grief, refusal, or defeat.
unlock: This is the posture of a person standing beside a decision they can't undo. Behind that door were lives still waiting to begin. In front of the door stood someone forced to leave them there.

title: Inventory
short: Two worker subjects perform an inventory of stacked seed cases. One records quantities. The other one verifies physical stock. They treat the content as critical.
unlock: This is what the end of the world looks like in its administrative phase. They weren't counting ordinary stock. They were counting how many chances humanity still had, and how few were left.

title: Warm Shaft
short: Three adult subjects positioned near an active vent or pipe assembly. The heat source appears industrial. Proximity suggests thermal dependency.
unlock: This vent wasn't built for this purpose. It served as a means to move heat, gas, pressure, and waste. Yet, people found warmth there, and so they gathered close to it. The human body doesn't care how it survives, only that it does. Toxic methane infrastructure became a shelter, not because its purpose changed, but because humans had nowhere kinder left to go.

title: Earthfire
short: One adult subject operates a small heat source. One child subject holds a food container over the heat. Heat appears to be exhaust from a vent or similar ground-linked system.
unlock: They're cooking over heat that should never have been there. The planet is leaking methane, the machine is failing to contain it, and so the ground gives off the wrong kind of warmth. Still, they decide to make a meal of it. This scene represents human survival at its smallest and strangest, when you can turn an aspect of global disaster into dinner. Hunger cannot wait for a better world.

title: Handoff
short: Two adult subjects positioned on separate floating objects. One adult subject transfers an  infant subject to the other across an open space.
unlock: There isn't a safe way to do what they're doing. Rowboats are highly sensitive to shifting weight and can easily capsize if their center of gravity moves too much to one side. However, the man's inflatable raft is failing, so they have no choice but to attempt a dangerous transfer. The real risk isn't the handover of the child. It's when he attempts to climb aboard. We don't know the end result of their actions.

title: Left Behind
short: An adult subject in distress in the water, swimming toward a floating raft. Two other adult subjects aboard the raft, moving it away from the swimmer.
unlock: Survivors never want to remember these moments. That raft can't hold three people. Allowing another person onboard means death for everyone. So, they must get the raft moving as fast as possible because neither survivor wishes to force the hands of the swimmer away. They don't look back either because looking would make the swimmer human again. The swimmer knows their decision too, but the body tries to survive anyway.

title: Debris
short: An adult subject and a child subject hold onto a floating object. No vessel detected. Open water surrounds the subjects.
unlock: The adult is holding both the child and the flotsam because holding both makes them all more stable. The flotsam was never meant to save anyone. It just happened to remain above water when everything else didn't.

title: Cargo Net
short: Three adult subjects climb a suspended net hanging on the side of a vessel. The subjects' show upward movement under physical strain.
unlock: Safety lies above them, but reaching it still requires one final effort. It's hard to watch. Safety is visible, but not yet reached. Exhausted arms, slippery hands, wet rope, and crowded bodies all work against them. Even at the edge of survival, people still had to climb.

title: Drift Pair
short: Two adult subjects on floating devices. They maintain a physical connection to each other.
unlock: They knew the water could quietly take one away from the other. So, they built a promise out of cloth. If one began to disappear, the other would feel it. They intend to survive together or die together.

title: Signal Wave
short: Three adult subjects stand on an unstable floating platform. Two of them perform repeated overhead signal motions with a fabric attached to a rod.
unlock: Without radios or flares, the human body itself must communicate the message. They're spending their last strength in an effort to be seen by a distant passing vessel. They wave desperately, not because they believe they'll be seen, but because not waving means surrendering to an eventual death.

title: Rooftop Wait
short: Three adult subject and one quadruped subject positioned on an elevated surface. All subjects are orienting towards the sea. Flood isolation evident.
unlock: The rooftop isn't their home, but it's the last part of their home still above water. They rescued the dog because love isn't practical. Unfortunately, the rest of the world is coldly practical. So, this family waits together on top of the highest piece of what used to be theirs, trying to make the area comfortable as they wait for a rescue that never comes.

title: Ladder Nowhere
short: One adult subject stabilizes a ladder structure. A second adult subject climbs upward while carrying a load, attempting to reach an elevated surface.
unlock: They're not climbing toward safety. Not yet. They're climbing to gain a little more time. When water claims all the ground, going up becomes the only escape. One person holds the ladder stable against the rushing water because trust is a survival necessity. If those hands fail, any future above falls into the water with them.

title: Window Exit
short: An adult subject transfers a child subject through an aperture opening to the exterior. The adult subject's head is oriented back toward the interior.
unlock: When life is ordinary, people enjoy the luxury of leaving through doors. Exiting a home through a window means the house has stopped obeying the normal rules of life. The adult helps the child escape first, but looks back because they must rush to leave their home forever. Home is still there, but it's not there. That's why abandoning home hurts.

title: Threshold
short: Two adult subjects positioned at an access point carrying transport cases. Both remain stationary. No exit movement detected.
unlock: They're staring out through the exit of their home. The door sits open, but leaving forever still requires one of them to step forward and make it real. They hesitate because that first step will divide their life into a before and after. Their suitcases are ready. Their home is ready to be abandoned. Yet, the people are still trying to belong to it a few more moments.

title: Last Plant
short: A single adult subject walks away from a residential structure carrying organic flora in a small container close to their center mass.
unlock: Evacuating with a potted plant isn't practical, but that's why it matters. This person carries the plant simply because it's alive and fits in their arms. And it comes from their home. When nothing about your home can be saved, one small green plant becomes proof that you still care and can make a difference.

title: Hallway Queue
short: Five human subjects of varying size arranged in a queue formation. Carried belongings detected. Group oriented toward a closed access point.
unlock: A waiting line is a promise that someone knows the proper order of things. That's what makes this scene tragic and cruel. Waiting in line is what civilized people do when they believe a system still exists. But those doors will remain closed, so now the line slowly transforms into their only way to avoid panic.

title: Shoulders
short: A child subject rides an adult subject. A second adult subject indicates a direction while carrying a load.
unlock:
The child is lifted above the crowd, above the water, above fear for as long as the adult can hold them.
It's a simple act of love; it's almost ordinary. That's why it hurts. Parents always lift their children to give them a higher perspective. They do it here so the child can see hope for a little longer.

title: Missing Bench
short: Single adult subject in a seated position. Two adjacent seats are occupied by carrying bags.
unlock: That person doesn't consider the seats to be empty. At least, not yet. They're being reserved and protected. They're being kept available for people who are permanently late. The bags say, "They're coming back." The person's posture says, "I know they're not."

title: Family Ledger
short: Four adult subjects positioned near a vertical, planar data receptacle. One subject holds paper. The remaining subjects point at the data receptacle.
unlock: They're trying to make a disaster more understandable. They're using a whiteboard to plan out their actions. By reducing a disaster down to names, routes, rooms, numbers, arrows... fear can be turned into a task. Humans do this whenever situations become too large to survive emotionally.

title: Balcony Hand-Off
short: Two adult subjects positioned on two elevated, separated scaffolds. One adult subject transfers an infant subject to the other across an open space.
unlock: The baby crosses first because the baby can't choose.  No safety exists here, just trust stretched across thin air. For one terrible second, the future is held by careful hands, not by systems, plans, or rescue orders.

title: Wheelchair
short: One adult subject pushing an elderly subject in a wheeled mobility device. Movement obstructed by uneven ground or debris.
unlock: The wheelchair slows down the evacuation effort, and that's exactly why this matters. Survival always rewards speed, strength, and the ability to maneuver. This person refuses that logic. Against all odds, they push, pull, lift, and keep the wheelchair steady.  When survival completely abandons the slow, vulnerable, and fragile, it has already lost an essential part of its purpose.

title: Roofline Argument
short: Three adult subjects positioned on an elevated structure. Two of the subjects indicate opposing directions. The third subject stands between them securing carrying bags.
unlock: Neither person is wrong enough to make the decision easy. The right direction could lead to rescue. The other direction might lead to shelter. Waiting could mean death. So does moving. Hope has split into two pointing hands. The person between them must choose which future to betray.

title: Cabinet Barricade
short: Two adult subjects apply force to a heavy object against an access point barrier. Action appears defensive. Access intentionally blocked.
unlock: They know the cabinet isn't enough to barricade the door by itself, but enough isn't available. Instead, they used whatever weight, wood, and metal was lying around, augmented by their fear. The furniture must serve as a wall because the real walls have failed them. The door is still a door. The room is still a room. But, safety has become something people must physically hold in place.

title: Returned Keys
short: Two adult human subjects in close exchange posture. A small access object is transferred from one subject's hand to the other's hand. The giver turns away.
unlock: Keys symbolize an eventual return. Here, they mean the opposite. One person is giving away a home, a room, a duty, or a life they can no longer keep. There's no ceremony for this kind of ending. Just a small piece of metal pressed into another person's hand. The former owner turns away before they can feel anything.

title: Bus Shelter
short: Three adult subjects seated beneath a transit shelter structure. They hold carrying bags on their laps. All the subjects orient their faces in the direction of the expected transit mod.
unlock: They sit the way people usually sit when a bus runs late. Unknown to them, the city has stopped keeping its promises. By habit, they still know how to wait inside a working system. Bags on laps. Eyes searching in anticipation. Hope reduced to the waiting posture of public transport passengers.

title: Dr. Evelyn James
short: Multiple human subjects gathered around an active data terminal. One adult subject appears to read while the others remain in postures listening.
unlock: They're listening in awe, but not because of the data being presented. They're awed by the fact that a single person carried the weight of humanity's first impossible decision. Evelyn James didn't save the world. No one did. But she saw the end coming and chose to build a path through it before most people even dared to look at the truth.

title: Looking At Rocket
short: Multiple human subjects positioned in the loading zone and facing the launch vehicle. The group remains stationary. Attention directed upward.
unlock: They're looking intently at more than just a rocket. They're looking at the only Exit door left on the Earth. Every person present feels the same hope to somehow be on that rocket. If it launches successfully, life continues. If it fails, death. And the future shrinks that much smaller for humanity. That's why no one is celebrating yet. Hope this large isn't light. It has weight. It makes people stand still as they await the final verdict.

title: Slip
short: Three human subjects at the edge of a platform. The smallest subject suffers instability. The two larger subjects reach out unsuccessfully for the falling subject.
unlock: No system is capable of properly cataloging this kind of tragic moment. It's not recorded as a policy failure nor as a launch failure. A child accidentally took one step in the wrong direction. Then, the child's guardians learn exactly how little time they have to save what they love.

title: Looking Over The Rail
short: Two adult subjects and two child subject positioned along a safety rail. All attention directed outward beyond the platform boundary.
unlock: They're not sightseeing. They're trying to gauge distance with their eyes. They're looking for a boat, a body, a sign, a reason, any indication that the ocean might give back something it had taken. Sometimes, people stand at a railing because they need to keep hoping and believing.

title: Inspection at Exodus
short: Multiple human subjects gathered inside a launch-associated structure. Their attention is directed toward major facility systems and the departure route to the launch.
unlock: They were trying to join the Exodus. They were supposed to be part of something that united humanity. Instead, what they truly witnessed was division. Who gets to ascend? Who had to stay behind? Who got to become part of the future, and who became forgotten memories? In a situation like this, a rocket is never just a vehicle. It's your final judgement made visible for everyone to see.

title: The Fire That Wouldn't Die
short: Four adult subjects around an industrial extraction unit. One adult subject is in direct physical contact with the machine. The other three subjects stand in a posture of observation and waiting.
unlock: They're not praying to a god. They're essentially praying to the machine because the machine still provides heat and fuel, at least for one more day. When a pump becomes something you beg, the world has already ended. It merely hasn't ended for you just yet.

title: FS-204-72 - Internal System Log
short: A Caretaker unit inserts a Lazarus Seed into a cryogenic preservation unit. Object handling indicates extreme fragility and high future-value priority.
unlock: This is the moment a life stopped being a child and became a responsibility sealed away until it could be used. They placed the unborn into the machine because the world could no longer hold them warm.

title: Oceanmark White
short: Multiple adult subjects gathered around a preserved white-coated surface. It's material condition remains unusually clean despite age and marine exposure.
unlock: They are looking at the coating like proof of success. White, clean, untouched by salt, water, rot, and even time. That was the promise of Oceanmark White. It helped the human world resist the ocean. Unfortunately, every painted surface survived too well. This coating didn't return to nature. It never broke down into more basic components. Surfaces only remained pristine because everything living in the surrounding biosphere paid the price.

title: Talon Shark ID-407
short: One bio-cybernetic shark subject positioned on a containment platform. One worker subject inserts a module into the shark subject. Termination posture detected.
unlock: The shark obeyed the command to die. That's the unbearable part. It was born and built to guard, hunt, return, and submit. When the final command came, it dutifully swam itself to the termination platform like a loyal machine. Yet, it wasn't just a machine. The operator understood too late that they created a living machine capable of suffering and feeling fear. Still, they required it to die according to protocol.

title: Smile in the Dark
short: One adult subject lies prone near a fuel container and emergency light source. A child subject stands nearby in a posture of observation and confusion.
unlock: The smile isn't due to happiness. It's the last tool left when medicine, rescue, and power have all failed. Another face smiles in that room. It becomes the boy's last comfort.

title: CS-17 Courier Skimmer
short: A Caretaker unit riding a courier vessel detected at Warehouse Alpha. The Caretaker sits in an endless standby mode. Delivery or return posture maintained.
unlock: The Caretaker came back because that's what it was programmed to do. It didn't know the dock was empty, or that the hands meant to load new cargo were gone. This is the tragedy of useful machines. They complete their journeys in perfection, yet still return too late.

title: Pig Experiment
short: Multiple porcine subjects contained in individual restraint frames. Movement restricted. Observation environment active. Human operators not visible.
unlock: They were not animals in the old sense anymore, and not people in the protected sense either. That was the gap MOSES used. The subjects learned pattern, fear, memory, and song, but the system only measured response. By the time anyone understood what the songs meant, the cages had already become the answer.

title: Bouncing Echoes
short: Multiple juvenile subjects engaged in ball-based recreational activity. Repeated jumping, throwing, and chasing motions detected.
unlock: The children are keeping the game going. They aren't ignoring the win conditions exactly. They're defeating them for a few seconds at a time. A bouncing ball makes the world feel alive. It promises endless motion: up, down, and back to the hand. Their laughter is small against this dying world. Yet, in that moment, they at least conquer their room.

title: Still Listening
short: Single adult subject detected inside cryogenic containment unit. External structural breach activity present. Filament growth surrounds the chamber perimeter. No surface communication response.

title: The Shape of Us
short: Humanoid figure engraved in stone. Body indicated by vertical lines, split lower limbs, extended arms, and circular head form.
unlock: A human form reduced to its simplest details: a head, a body, four limbs, and a stance. Orbit stores this as early proof that humans wanted to see themselves from an exterior viewpoint. Before cameras, mirrors, or archives, they recorded the shape of being human.

title: Among Larger Lives
short: Four humanoid shapes appear near one quadruped organism and one tall biped organism. Pigment recorded on irregular stone surface.
unlock: Humans placed themselves beside animals larger, faster, and stranger than themselves. Orbit reads this as observation and humility. Early humanity understood that it lived among other powerful beings, and that survival began by watching them carefully.

title: Before Words
short: Multiple straight and undulating lines recorded on stone. Pattern is non-random, but intended meaning is unresolved.
unlock: A human hand sought to make their cave feel less empty. These marks may have carried meanings related to signs, paths, names, warnings, or memory. Orbit records the deeper lesson: humans used stone to store meaning that could live beyond the moment it was born.

title: Inside the Living Pattern
short: Several animal-like forms and human-like forms drawn with pigment on stone. Figures appear grouped in shared space.
unlock: Humans recorded animals, not as background but as part of the same world. Food, danger, weather, movement, and memory may all be present here. Orbit stores the image as evidence that early humans learned about the natural world by placing themselves inside the living patterns that they could perceive.

title: Many Around the Herd
short: Dozens of simplified humanoid silhouettes positioned around multiple animal-like organisms.
unlock: Many bodies. Many animals. Shared movement across stone. Orbit records this as a memory of group life: humans hunting, watching, following, gathering, and acting together. The image survives because the need to communicate this information outlived the people who made it.

title: Wall of Shared Signs
short: An arrangement of animal-like and human-like shapes interspersed with patterned geometric marks, loose forms, and lines.
unlock: This image appears to gather several kinds of knowledge into one place: bodies, animals, marks, movement, and possible tools. Orbit will store it as an early memory wall, where practical life and meaning were recorded together. The full explanation is gone, but the purpose remains clear enough. Important information was placed here so it could be learned again.

title: Facing Bison
short: Two large quadruped organisms of considerable mass positioned close to one another.
unlock: Two bison face each other with enough care that the meeting itself becomes the memory. Orbit records this as sustained attention toward powerful animals, noting their weight, posture, danger, and importance. Early humans learned survival by watching other life closely, then they wrote that knowledge into stone so it wouldn't end with one hunter or one season.

title: The Marked Hand
short: A pattern of incised marks on a stone surface shaped like an isolated humanoid right hand.
unlock: A human hand was turned into a lasting mark. The dense internal pattern suggests the maker was not just showing a body part, but making the hand carry identity, memory, or claim. Orbit stores this as an early declaration of presence. The artist used stone to say that a living person had touched the world and wanted recognition for it.

title: Animals in Motion
short: A grouping of animal-like quadruped shapes. Several directional marks intersect or point toward their bodies.
unlock: This image preserves animals in motion. Orbit reads it as a human attempt to portray a moment where life, food, fear, and skill intersected. They show humans needing to remember an intense experience that will likely be repeated in their lives.

title: Work Beneath the Tree
short: Five humanoid figures. One figure is connected to vertical lines. Four figures are arranged below in an approximate circular formation.
unlock: This scene records humans in food-gathering activities. A figure climbs a tree, while hunters are grouped below. Overall, the image suggests shared work, observation, risk, or instruction. Orbit stores this as evidence that early human memory was social. A task was not only remembered as actions, but as roles among people.

title: Circle of the Herd
short: Four zoomorphic shapes drawn around a patterned circle with a curving line above the group.
unlock: The animals are placed in relation to a larger symbol, possibly connected to place, season, sky, route, or gathering point. Orbit cannot confirm the system, but the arrangement shows practical intelligence. Early humans weren't merely recording creatures. They were recording the conditions under which life was found, followed, feared, or used.

title: Hands Among the Animals
short: Humanoid shapes arranged around a group of animal-like forms. One human hand appears integrated into the same image field.
unlock: Hands placed among animals is an act that joins the human body to the living world around it. Orbit records this as a memory of presence and relation. Humans saw these creatures, depended on them, feared them, and marked themselves beside them. The image teaches that early humanity understood itself through the other lives it shared the world with.

title: What the Water Gave
short: Two large ichthyomorphic shapes drawn in x-ray with internal structure. Several bipedal human-like figures are positioned around them.
unlock: This image records humans living from water, not only looking at it. The fish are shown with their insides known, suggesting food, anatomy, memory, and skill were connected. Orbit stores this as evidence that early humans learned survival by reading the bodies of other life, then preserved that knowledge for the group.

title: Among Larger Bodies
short: Dozens of animal-like organisms surround two human-like figures. Human forms appear small relative to the animal group.
unlock: Humans placed themselves inside a field of powerful animals. Orbit reads this as a memory of scale, small bodies moving among larger, stronger beings. The image preserves more than the idea of prey. It shows that early humans understood danger as something to study, approach together, and turn into shared knowledge.

title: What Could Feed and Wound
short: A single animal-like quadruped form recorded on stone.
unlock: A wild boar that an artist chose to preserve. Orbit records this as a need to instruct others about the important details of the animal, such as its appearance, shape, weight, danger, and usefulness. Early humans respected the sources of their food. What could feed them could also wound them, and that made it valuable to remember.

title: A Figure That Outlived Its Maker
short: Human-like figure formed from repeated carved marks on a stone surface.
unlock: Human shapes carved into an enduring form. Orbit will archive this art as an instance of early self-recognition. The image may carry meanings related to ceremony, danger, teaching, or play. Yet, the central lesson remains clear. Artists portrayed their bodies in a form that could outlive them.

title: A Bird Worth Remembering
short: An ornithomorphic shape drawn using engraved marks.
unlock: The artist observed a wild turkey closely enough to preserve it as a detailed memory. Orbit records this art as an instructional communication about food, movement, and season. Early humans passed down survival skills by using cave paintings such as these to educate others, track animals, or map their environments.

title: A Map Before Maps
short: Interconnected irregular lines form closed loops, crossing paths, and branching channels.

title: A Small Model of the World
short: Cluster of carved symbolic shapes with circular forms, crescent forms, comb-like forms, and one human-like figure.
unlock: Several kinds of meaning are gathered into one surface: circles, curved signs, repeated marks, and a human body. Orbit records this as a compact world model. Early humans weren't only marking objects. They combined the sky, land, water, and themselves into a unified cosmology that they could remember and share.

title: The Wall That Kept Adding Life
short: Three intertwined zoomorphic quadruped shapes drawn in a stipple style.
unlock: Several animals occupy the same space, their bodies overlapping without losing their individual presence. Orbit records this as a layered work. The artist repeatedly returned to the same wall, or the same memory, and kept adding to it. The image teaches that early artwork didn't always depict a single event. Rather, it could be a place where many observations accumulated over time.

title: One Who Stood Different
short: Abstract line-carved figure composed of hatched, dashed, and branching marks. Central form appears upright and visually dominant.
unlock: A single figure that has been given structure, weight, and attention. Orbit records this as a possible image of a prominent role, someone marked as different, central, or wielding authority inside the group. Regardless if the image was portraying ceremony, warning, leadership, or transformation, the lesson is useful. Humans didn't just use art to only remember animals and places. They also defined social hierarchy.

title: The Calling Stag
short: Single animal-like quadruped shown in profile. Branching forms extend from the head area. Neck and head are raised.
unlock: A stag is shown calling out during mating season. Its head is raised. The antlers are extended. The bellow is implied. Orbit records this as humans learning about seasonal life. Animals weren't just resources. Their posture, timing, and behavior told humans when the world was changing, and how survival had to change with it.

title: Skill Passed Forward
short: Two simplified forms linked by a vertical rod-like element. One form is dark and solid. One patterned circular mark appears near the connection.
unlock: This image depicts an encounter between an aboriginal hunter and a kangaroo or wallaby. Orbit believes it to be instructional. The painting shows how to track the animal's movement and aim a barbed spear. The lesson isn't just about the hunt. Early humans understood the need to preserve and communicate their skills from one person to another.

title: Knowledge in Pieces
short: Four separated image fields containing geometric marks, one striped animal-like form, one human-like form, and a structured dot matrix.
unlock: The image divides knowledge into parts, as if different observations belonged to one larger system. Animal, person, pattern, and mapped marks are held together. Orbit records this as organized memory. Early humans broke the world down into understandable pieces so others could understand where to go, what to watch for, and what was important.

title: Moving as One
short: Multiple animal-like quadruped forms interspersed with human-like figures. Figures appear distributed across a shared movement field.
unlock: Many humans and animals move through the same recorded space. Orbit reads this as group coordination. The humans are collectively hunting together. The image teaches that human survival was rarely solitary. Knowledge wasn't useful unless everyone understood it and acted in unison.

title: The Social Wall
short: Large collection of scattered symbols and line drawings. Elements include rows of vertical marks, circular forms, outlines, and human-like figures.
unlock: This wall gathers many small acts into one social field. Boundaries, bodies, repeated marks, and circular signs suggest a clan or tribe trying to preserve the memory of itself. Orbit records this as evidence that human life wasn't only about hunting or survival. People also organized living spaces, repeated customs, played, gathered, taught, and built a community.

title: Strength Remembered
short: Single animal-like quadruped shown in profile. Large shoulder mass, lowered head, and compact body form recorded.
unlock: An ancient bison remembered through its posture, heavy shoulders, and lowered head. It's a portrayal of contained strength and power. Orbit records this as a representation of an animal that humans both depended on and feared. The purpose of the image goes beyond just presenting its appearance. It shows early humans studied these animals carefully because understanding often made the difference between feeding the tribe and starvation.

title: What the Water Held
short: Five large fish-like patterned forms positioned near one human-like figure.
unlock: Large fish-like forms are placed beside a human body, making scale part of the memory. Orbit records this as knowledge of water, food, movement, and dependence. Whether these forms represent fish, vessels, or a larger water system, the lesson is that humans learned to survive by understanding life beneath the waters.

title: Thought Becomes Code
short: Horizontal row of seven distinct graphic symbols. Forms are separated, repeated, and visually ordered.
unlock: This image shows thought becoming code. The marks no longer need to resemble a body or animal in order to communicate meaning. Orbit stores it as an early step toward shared symbols. Humans realized that a simple sign could hold memory, instruction, identity, or warning long after the speaker was gone.

title: Environmental Memory
short: Scattered groups of animal-like quadruped forms with internal line shading. Additional unclear marks appear around the main figures.
unlock: Animals are placed inside a wider field of marks, suggesting that the creatures were remembered together with place, shelter, route, or event in mind. Orbit records this as an environmental memory. Early humans didn't think of life as separate from the world around it. They connected animals to the spaces and conditions relevant to survival.

title: Between Human and Animal
short: Three animal-like or hybrid forms arranged together. Two forms are large quadrupeds. One smaller upright form appears nearby.
unlock: The upright figure and the animals share the same symbolic space. Orbit records this as evidence that humans imagined a connection between their bodies and power over animals. The image teaches that survival wasn't just understood in practical terms. Humans also created stories, roles, and transformations to understand forces larger than themselves.

title: Feelings Made Visible
short: Jagged interconnected line formations arranged horizontally across stone. Several forms suggest stretched or reclining human-like figures.

title: The Shared Pursuit
short: Human-like figures appear on one side of the image field. Animal-like quadruped forms appear on the other. Several directional marks intersect the animals.
unlock: Humans and deer are shown inside a moment of pursuit, pressure, and consequence. Orbit records this as coordinated survival. Bodies are moving, aiming, fleeing, and falling within one shared event. The image teaches that early human memory held both skill and cost. The animals being pierced by lines means the hunters were shooting them with arrows. Food was never separate from the life taken to obtain it.

title: Branching Thought
short: Collection of abstract branching lines arranged across stone. Forms split, reconnect, and extend in multiple directions.
unlock: This image shows thought moving beyond bodies and animals into abstract patterns. Orbit stores it as evidence that humans learned to preserve relationships, paths, movement, or ideas without drawing the thing itself. The lesson is important. Abstraction allows memory to become lighter, stranger, and more portable.

title: Strength in Stone
short: Large animal-like quadruped form recorded in stone. Heavy shoulder mass, thick neck, and lowered head are visible.
unlock: A bison carved into permanent record. Orbit archives this image as sustained focus upon this animal's strength, weight, and danger. It may have also carried meanings related to food, fear, reverence, or instruction. The deeper lesson remains clear: early humans studied the lives of animals because preserving knowledge meant surviving another season more easily.

title: Early Code
short: Collection of abstract signs, symbolic forms, and animal-like shapes scattered across stone.
unlock: This surface holds several kinds of memory at once: animals, signs, repeated marks, and unresolved symbols. Orbit records it as early coded thinking. Humans were learning that meaning didn't always require a full picture. A mark could be used to symbolize food, route, warning, group, or story.

title: Learning to Belong
short: Multiple human-like figures of varying sizes and body shapes arranged together.
unlock: Humans are recorded here as a group, not as isolated bodies. Small and large figures share the same space, suggesting age, care, play, family, or teaching. Orbit stores this as evidence that early human life wasn't only about survival against the world. It also portrayed people learning how to belong to each other.

title: The Shared Scene
short: Multiple human-like figures appear among animal-like and structural shapes. One central animal-like form is large and upright.
unlock: This image gathers people, animals, and structures into one shared scene. Orbit records it as a community memory. It combines food, shelter, and people gathering. The lesson is that humans understood life as an interconnection of beings, activities, and places that cannot be reduced into discrete objects.

title: Mountain of Resources
short: Large proboscidean quadruped shown in side profile. Extended front appendage and large projecting head structures are visible.
unlock: The image shows an outline sketch of a woolly mammoth, portrayed as enormous and powerful. Orbit interprets the drawing as carrying meanings tied to food, danger, resources, tools, and ultimately awe. Early humans preserved the memories of encounters that mattered the most. In this case, a woolly mammoth represented a mountain of resources and therefore stood for a hunt that was both highly desirable and extremely dangerous.

title: Speed Remembered
short: Large animal-like quadruped shown in motion. Raised limbs suggest running or forward movement.
unlock: A horse remembered because of its speed. Orbit interprets this image as humans studying movement itself. The horse's legs are lifted. Its body is extended. Life is passing quickly through space. The image teaches that early human artwork didn't record just the appearances of animals. They also recorded how animals lived and moved through the natural world.

title: Beasts in Conflict
short: A cluster of stylized bovine and feline animals overlapping chaotic crosshatch markings.
unlock: This painting shows cave lions interacting with bison. The crosshatch markings could represent boundaries or hunter traps. Prehistoric humans used art to process their experiences and memories. This work reflects deep psychological connection with the natural world.

title: The Moving Plain
short: Horizontal row of overlapping proboscidean, bovine, and equine animals.
unlock: A procession of ice-age megafauna moving across a flat plain. They include mammoths, bison, and wild horses. Drawing the animals with overlapping outlines seems to be technique prehistoric artists used to capture the fleeting, kinetic motion of life.

title: The Marked Horse
short: A stylized equine animal with wavy lines extending from its chest and neck area.

title: The Bond Remembered
short: Minimalist line drawing of two proboscidean animals, a large and small one.
unlock: This image shows a mother mammoth and her calf. By showing the two animals moving forward together, the artist demonstrated an understanding of the kinship bond between them. Family relationships and the continuity of life across generations were recognized as meaningful.
